museum.name: Gaming Time Machine
museum.dek: May sits just before several foundational computer and arcade releases make 1981 feel like a turning point.
museum.curatorNote: May is mostly atmosphere and anticipation; exact month-level anchors remain thin.

sections.title: News
sections.subtitle: Five researched moments, with broad context separated from confident month-level claims.

items.title: Character arcade design is about to shift
items.body: Donkey Kong and Frogger are close, but this drawer keeps them in later month entries.
items.sourceNote: Donkey Kong and Frogger release context.

items.title: Apple II game culture deepens
items.body: The machine is becoming a home for RPGs, adventures and more ambitious disk-based games.
items.sourceNote: Ultima and Wizardry context.

items.title: Arcade routes favour instantly readable games
items.body: Operators need cabinets that pull in both expert players and curious passers-by.
items.sourceNote: Arcade-market context.

items.title: UK home computing remains partly aspirational
items.body: Many readers encounter games first as screenshots, listings and adverts rather than purchased tapes.
items.sourceNote: UK microcomputer context.

items.title: No confident May-only release anchor
items.body: Rather than invent a May launch shelf, the exhibit marks the evidential gap.
items.sourceNote: No confident May-specific release found.

sections.title: Releases
sections.subtitle: A cautious year-context shelf: no major month-specific release is forced for this drawer.

items.title: Scramble
items.body: Konami's forced-scroll shooter helps define the language of side-scrolling arcade stages.
items.sourceNote: US date cited by Arcade-History; Japan timing is often given more broadly as 1981.

items.title: Gorf
items.body: Midway's multi-stage shooter shows the arcade market turning hit formulas into theatrical cabinets.
items.sourceNote: Month is widely cited for the US arcade release.

items.title: Ultima
items.body: Richard Garriott's fantasy computer RPG begins a series that will shape PC role-playing for decades.
items.sourceNote: Official release usually cited as June 1981.

items.title: Centipede
items.body: Atari's trackball shooter becomes one of the year's defining cabinets; exact month differs by source.
items.sourceNote: MobyGames cites June; other summaries cite August.

items.title: Donkey Kong
items.body: Nintendo's construction-site platform drama introduces Jumpman and gives character action a new shape.
items.sourceNote: July 1981 is secure; regional day-level dates vary.

items.title: Frogger
items.body: A road-and-river crossing game with a clear little hero, instantly readable peril and a tune that stuck.
items.sourceNote: August appears in series summaries; use cautious month wording.

items.title: Wizardry: Proving Grounds of the Mad Overlord
items.body: A first-person dungeon crawl whose parties, stats and mapping rituals become computer-RPG grammar.
items.sourceNote: MobyGames and common references cite September 1981.

items.title: Castle Wolfenstein
items.body: Sneaking, disguises and procedural rooms make it an early ancestor of stealth games.
items.sourceNote: Several summaries cite September 1981; MobyGames gives 1981.

sections.title: Hardware
sections.subtitle: Four machines or contexts explaining how this month sat inside the wider technology culture.

items.title: Sinclair ZX81
items.body: A 1KB low-cost computer that makes programming and simple games feel newly reachable in Britain.
items.sourceNote: Launched in the UK in March 1981.

items.title: IBM Personal Computer 5150
items.body: Introduced in August, initially more office object than gaming dream, but historically enormous.
items.sourceNote: Introduced on 12 August 1981.

items.title: Atari VCS / 2600
items.body: The key home-console presence for many households before the market turns unstable.
items.sourceNote: Used as broad home-console context.

items.title: Arcade cabinets
items.body: Custom controls, bright marquees and operator placement still define the public videogame experience.
items.sourceNote: Used as broad arcade-culture context.

sections.title: Online Life
sections.subtitle: Before online gaming was ordinary, print and place carried the culture.

items.title: Online life is mostly absent from ordinary play
items.body: For most players, discovery happens through arcades, shops, magazines, friends and mail order.

items.title: Bulletin boards exist, but not as mass games culture
items.body: Computer enthusiasts may know modems and BBS culture, but it is not yet the everyday route into games.

items.title: Print is the network
items.body: A new magazine can connect owners of machines that would otherwise feel isolated.

items.title: The UK conversation is local
items.body: School friends, electronics shops and newsagents carry more practical information than any online service.

sections.title: What It Felt Like
sections.subtitle: A short atmospheric reading of the month as a player might have met it.

items.title: The arcade was still the brightest room
items.body: May 1981 still belongs to public machines: glowing marquees, cigarette-smudged cabinets, coins lined up on glass and the pressure of playing while someone watched.

items.title: Home computing felt like an invitation
items.body: A computer at home was not just a games machine. It was a thing you typed into, misunderstood, crashed and slowly learned.

items.title: The future had several shapes
items.body: A videogame could be a cabinet, a cartridge, a disk, a printed listing or a tiny black-and-white program saved to cassette.

items.title: In Britain, the door was opening
items.body: The ZX81 made the idea of owning a computer feel less impossible, even if the games were still austere and the keyboard unforgiving.
